verses.text: The sons of the prophets, outgrowing their lodging, go to cut wood by the Jordan with Elisha. The work is humble and ordinary. God's prophets are not above manual labour; faith does not despise the plain tasks of daily provision.

The sons of the prophets, outgrowing their lodging, go to cut wood by the Jordan with Elisha. The work is humble and ordinary. God's prophets are not above manual labour; faith does not despise the plain tasks of daily provision.

A borrowed axe head flies off into the water, and the man cries out in distress; Elisha casts in a stick and the iron floats. God cares about a poor man's small loss. No trouble is too trivial to bring to Him, who can recover even what sinks beyond reach.

A borrowed axe head flies off into the water, and the man cries out in distress; Elisha casts in a stick and the iron floats. God cares about a poor man's small loss. No trouble is too trivial to bring to Him, who can recover even what sinks beyond reach.

When Syria plans ambushes, Elisha repeatedly warns Israel's king of the enemy's secret moves. God reveals to His prophet what no spy could learn. Nothing whispered in the councils of the wicked is hidden from the LORD or beyond His power to disclose.

When Syria plans ambushes, Elisha repeatedly warns Israel's king of the enemy's secret moves. God reveals to His prophet what no spy could learn. Nothing whispered in the councils of the wicked is hidden from the LORD or beyond His power to disclose.

When Syria plans ambushes, Elisha repeatedly warns Israel's king of the enemy's secret moves. God reveals to His prophet what no spy could learn. Nothing whispered in the councils of the wicked is hidden from the LORD or beyond His power to disclose.

Furious, the Syrian king learns it is Elisha and sends a great army by night to seize one prophet at Dothan. The world fears the man of God more than armies. They surround a single servant with horses and chariots, as if he were a nation.

Elisha's servant panics at the besieging host, but the prophet prays, 'open his eyes,' and he sees the mountain full of horses and chariots of fire. 'They that be with us are more.' Faith sees the unseen armies of God that ring His people round.

Elisha prays the Syrians be struck blind, leads the whole army into Samaria, then asks God to open their eyes — and they stand trapped in the enemy capital. God turns the hunters into the helpless. The plot to capture the prophet delivers them into his hand.

Elisha prays the Syrians be struck blind, leads the whole army into Samaria, then asks God to open their eyes — and they stand trapped in the enemy capital. God turns the hunters into the helpless. The plot to capture the prophet delivers them into his hand.

Elisha prays the Syrians be struck blind, leads the whole army into Samaria, then asks God to open their eyes — and they stand trapped in the enemy capital. God turns the hunters into the helpless. The plot to capture the prophet delivers them into his hand.

Instead of killing them, Elisha has them fed a great feast and sent home, and the Syrian raids cease for a time. Mercy disarms the enemy more than slaughter would. Kindness to foes can shame their hostility and win a peace the sword could not.

Later Ben-hadad besieges Samaria till famine is so dire a donkey's head sells for silver and dung for food. The horror of the siege is unsparing. Sin's long harvest can bring a people to extremity, and Scripture does not soften the misery it records.

A woman cries to the king — she and another agreed to eat their sons, and now the other hides hers. The famine has driven mothers to unspeakable evil. When a people abandons God, the breakdown reaches even the deepest bonds of nature and love.

The king tears his clothes, shows sackcloth beneath, yet vows to behead Elisha, blaming the prophet for the LORD's judgment. He has the form of grief without repentance. It is easier to rage at God's messenger than to turn from the sin that brought the ruin.
